title: Elmer Gantry
description: A satirical novel about evangelical hypocrisy, ambition, and the business of religion.
descriptionParagraphs: A satirical novel about evangelical hypocrisy, ambition, and the business of religion.

Elmer Gantry is usually read through its treatment of religion, satire, and fraud. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes religion, satire, and fraud feel immediate.

overview.englishDescription: A satirical novel about evangelical hypocrisy, ambition, and the business of religion.
banningParagraphs: Elmer Gantry entered censorship debates as a novel associated with religion, satire, and fraud. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around blasphemy and anti clericalism.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1920s-1930s in United States, where Municipal and customs authorities banned and challenged. Lewis's portrait of religious fraud provoked both legal challenges and moral outrage. Its censorship history sits at the boundary of blasphemy and literary satire.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
